original_hypothesis.title: cancel() Missing Authorization Allows Anyone to Cancel Any Order
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The cancel() function checks only that order.maker != address(0) (order exists) but never verifies msg.sender == order.maker. The Unauthorized error is defined but never used. Any external caller can cancel any active order. While funds are refunded to the original maker, this enables systematic orderbook destruction: an attacker can cancel every order in the DEX, disrupting all trading activity at near-zero cost.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The Unauthorized error is declared at line level but never referenced in any function. cancel() delegates to _cancelOrder which also lacks authorization. This is a clear missing access control bug.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cancel() checks order.maker != address(0) but never checks msg.sender == order.maker; error Unauthorized() defined but unused

preconditions: At least one active order exists in the orderbook
Attacker can call cancel() with the target orderId

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Any caller can cancel any order, destroying orderbook liquidity
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No authorization check despite Unauthorized error being defined

analysis_questions: Can an attacker enumerate all order IDs to cancel the entire orderbook?
Does the refund to maker create any secondary issues?
Can this be weaponized to prevent fills by cancelling orders before they execute?
validation_requirements: Call cancel(orderId) from non-maker address, verify it succeeds
Verify funds are returned to maker, not caller

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: 1. Attacker monitors on-chain confirmed orders via getOrders(). 2. Attacker iterates through orderHeads for known token pairs. 3. Attacker calls cancel() for each active orderId. 4. All orders cancelled - DEX becomes unusable. 5. Attack can be repeated indefinitely at gas cost only.
prerequisites: Knowledge of active order IDs (publicly readable via getOrders)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: No direct fund theft but complete DoS of trading functionality
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All market makers and traders on the DEX
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Orders can be re-placed but attacker can re-cancel in perpetuity

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for cancel() called by non-maker address

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: ~50K gas per cancel, affordable for wiping orderbook
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed at any time
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as mass cancellation events

validation_result.impact_summary: Any external caller can cancel any active order in the DEX. While funds are refunded to the original maker, this enables complete orderbook destruction and denial of service.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The cancel() function at StablecoinDEX.sol lines 217-221 checks only that order.maker != address(0) (i.e., the order exists) but never verifies that msg.sender == order.maker. The _cancelOrder() internal function (lines 228-258) also contains no authorization check. The error `Unauthorized()` is defined at line 131 but is never referenced anywhere in the contract. This means any address can call cancel(orderId) for any active order. The refund of locked tokens (order.remaining) is credited to the original maker's balance, so no direct fund theft occurs. However, this constitutes a critical missing access control defect that enables systematic griefing of all market participants.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is accurate. Lines 217-221 show the cancel function with only an existence check. Line 131 defines `error Unauthorized();` which is never used. The _cancelOrder function at lines 228-258 performs the cancellation without any sender verification. The code path from cancel() -> _cancelOrder() has zero authorization gates.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function checks only that the order exists (maker != address(0)) but never checks msg.sender == order.maker. Any external caller can cancel any active order.
Internal function also lacks any authorization check. Refunds are sent to the original maker, confirming no direct theft, but the order is destroyed without the maker's consent.
The Unauthorized error is defined but never used anywhere in the contract, strongly indicating that an authorization check was intended but never implemented.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls cancel(orderId) where orderId belongs to another user's active order
2. cancel() loads Order storage at orders[orderId] (line 218)
3. cancel() checks order.maker != address(0) — passes because order is active (line 219)
4. cancel() calls _cancelOrder(orderId, order) — NO msg.sender check (line 221)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire cancel() -> _cancelOrder() path has no authorization gate. The only check is order existence (maker != address(0)). An attacker can enumerate all orders via getOrders() (line 375-395 provides order IDs for any token pair) and call cancel() on each one. The getOrderHead() function at line 357 and getOrders() at line 375 make order discovery trivial.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that would affect this finding. The vulnerability is inherent in the contract code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is fully valid: 1) Attacker calls getOrders(baseToken, quoteToken, maxOrders) for known token pairs to enumerate all active order IDs. 2) For each orderId, attacker calls cancel(orderId). 3) Each call succeeds because only order existence is checked. 4) All orders are cancelled, refunds go to original makers. 5) The DEX orderbook is completely wiped. 6) Cost is only gas (~50K per cancel). 7) Attack can be repeated as new orders are placed, making the DEX permanently unusable. Additionally, an attacker can front-run fillOrder transactions by cancelling the target order before the fill executes, causing the fill to revert with OrderDoesNotExist.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. While no direct fund theft occurs (refunds go to makers), the impact is: (1) Complete denial of service — entire orderbook can be wiped at minimal cost. (2) Front-running fills — orders can be cancelled before fills execute. (3) Market maker griefing — makes providing liquidity impossible. (4) DEX becomes permanently unusable if attacker runs a bot. The defined but unused `Unauthorized` error strongly indicates this was an oversight, not intentional design.
recommended_mitigations: Add authorization check in cancel(): if (msg.sender != order.maker) revert Unauthorized();
Alternatively, add the check in _cancelOrder() if it could be called from other internal paths in the future
Consider adding a mapping of orderId => maker for O(1) lookup if gas optimization is needed
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StablecoinDEX and a mock ERC20 token. 2. From address A: deposit tokens, place an order. 3. From address B (attacker): call cancel(orderId) where orderId is A's order. 4. Verify the call succeeds without revert. 5. Verify order is cancelled and tokens refunded to A's balance. 6. Demonstrate full orderbook wipe by creating multiple orders from different addresses and cancelling all from a single attacker address.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test: attempt cancel from random addresses, verify it always succeeds for active orders
Integration test: simulate front-running a fillOrder by cancelling the target order in the same block
